name: Mapo Tofu
description: Sichuan's most electrifying dish — silken tofu cubes in a fiery, numbing sauce of doubanjiang, Sichuan peppercorns, and minced pork. The name means 'pockmarked old lady's tofu' — and it's perfect.
instructions: Gently simmer tofu cubes in salted water 2 minutes; drain carefully.
Heat oil in a wok on high. Fry minced pork until browned and broken up.
Add doubanjiang and fermented black beans; fry 2 minutes until oil turns red.
Add garlic and ginger; stir 1 minute.
Pour in stock and soy sauce. Bring to a simmer.
Gently slide in tofu. Simmer 3 minutes, spooning sauce over without stirring.
Stir in cornflour mixture to thicken. Drizzle chilli oil, dust with Sichuan pepper, scatter spring onions. Serve over rice.
tips: Don't stir the tofu — spoon the sauce over it instead to keep cubes intact. Sichuan peppercorns should be freshly ground for the numbing tingle.

name: Hot and Sour Soup
description: A Chinese restaurant classic with bold contrasting flavours — vinegar-spiked broth with lily buds, wood ear mushrooms, tofu, and silky egg ribbons, thickened to a satisfying consistency.
instructions: Bring stock to boil. Add pork strips; simmer 2 minutes.
Add mushrooms, lily buds, bamboo shoots. Simmer 3 minutes.
Add tofu. Season with soy sauce, vinegar, white pepper, and salt.
Stir in cornflour slurry gradually until soup reaches a slightly thick, flowing consistency.
While stirring gently in circles, drizzle beaten eggs in a thin stream to create ribbons.
Stir in sesame oil. Ladle into bowls; drizzle chilli oil and scatter spring onions.
tips: Add vinegar at the end of cooking for brightest flavour — it mellows when boiled too long. White pepper is non-negotiable here.

name: Sweet and Sour Pork (Gu Lao Rou)
description: Crispy battered pork tossed in a glossy sweet-sour sauce with bell peppers, onion, and pineapple — the Cantonese original that inspired countless adaptations worldwide.
instructions: Marinate pork with salt, white pepper, and soy sauce 15 minutes.
Whisk egg into pork, then coat with cornflour. Fry in 170°C oil until golden; drain. Fry a second time at 190°C for 1 minute for extra crisp.
Mix sauce: ketchup, vinegar, sugar, pineapple juice, and 2 tbsp water. Stir to dissolve.
In a wok, stir-fry garlic, onion, and bell peppers on high heat 2 minutes.
Add pineapple, then pour in sauce. Bring to a bubble.
Toss in crispy pork immediately. Coat quickly and serve at once so the pork stays crispy.
tips: Double-frying is the key to pork that stays crispy in the sauce. Add the pork to the sauce just before serving.

name: Pork Spring Rolls
description: Crunchy golden wrappers filled with a fragrant stir-fry of pork, vermicelli, cabbage, and mushrooms — a Lunar New Year staple symbolising prosperity, best dipped in sweet chilli sauce.
instructions: Stir-fry garlic and pork in a hot wok until cooked. Add mushrooms, carrot, and cabbage.
Season with soy sauce, oyster sauce, five spice, and sesame oil. Stir-fry until vegetables are wilted.
Fold in glass noodles and spring onions. Cool filling completely before wrapping.
Place 2 tbsp filling near one corner of a wrapper. Fold corner over, fold in sides, roll tightly. Seal with beaten egg.
Fry rolls in 170°C oil, turning, until deep golden and crispy — about 4 minutes.
Drain and serve with sweet chilli sauce or soy-vinegar dip.
tips: Cool the filling completely before wrapping — hot filling makes soggy rolls. Don't overfill.

name: Pan-Fried Pork Dumplings (Jiaozi)
description: China's most celebrated bite — hand-pleated dumplings filled with pork and cabbage, pan-fried to crispy golden bottoms and served with a ginger-soy-vinegar dipping sauce.
instructions: Make dough: combine flour with hot water then cold water. Knead 10 minutes until smooth. Rest covered 30 minutes.
Mix filling: pork, cabbage, ginger, chives, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, sesame oil, and white pepper.
Roll dough into a cylinder; cut into 30 pieces. Roll each into thin 8cm circles.
Place 1 tsp filling in centre. Fold in half and pleat the edge 4–5 times to seal.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan. Place dumplings flat-side down; fry 2 minutes until golden.
Pour in 1/3 cup water; cover immediately. Steam 6 minutes until water evaporates.
Remove lid, cook 1 more minute. Serve with soy-vinegar-ginger dip.
tips: The pleating takes practice — even if yours are rustic, they taste amazing. Squeeze the cabbage very dry or filling will leak.

name: Dan Dan Noodles
description: Sichuan's famous street noodles in a sesame-chilli sauce spiked with Sichuan peppercorns, topped with a dry-fried pork and preserved mustard greens mixture, with a pool of chilli oil.
instructions: Make the sauce: whisk together tahini, sesame paste, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and stock until smooth.
Add garlic, ginger, Sichuan pepper, and chilli oil. Taste and adjust.
Dry-fry pork in a hot pan until crispy. Add Yacai; fry 2 more minutes. Season with 1 tbsp soy sauce.
Cook noodles per packet instructions; drain.
Divide sauce between bowls. Top with hot noodles and blanched bok choy.
Spoon crispy pork mixture over noodles. Drizzle more chilli oil; scatter spring onions. Toss before eating.
tips: Authentic dan dan noodles are barely sauced — the components are mixed at the table. Chilli oil quality is everything.

name: Char Siu (Cantonese BBQ Pork)
description: The jewel of Cantonese cuisine — pork shoulder glazed with a honey-hoisin-five spice marinade and roasted until caramelised and lacquered deep red. The backbone of wonton noodle soup and fried rice.
instructions: Mix marinade: hoisin, honey, soy sauce, wine, five spice, garlic, sugar, and red yeast rice powder.
Reserve 3 tbsp marinade for glazing. Coat pork strips in remaining marinade; refrigerate overnight (minimum 4 hours).
Preheat oven to 200°C. Place pork on a rack over a foil-lined tray of water.
Roast 20 minutes. Brush with reserved marinade glaze.
Flip, brush again, and roast 15 more minutes.
Switch to grill/broil for 3–4 minutes until edges caramelise and char slightly.
Slice and serve with steamed rice and blanched Chinese greens.
tips: The overnight marinade develops the deep colour. The last broil blast is what gives authentic char siu its glossy, slightly charred edges.

name: Wonton Soup
description: A Cantonese soul warmer — silky thin-skinned wontons filled with pork and shrimp floating in a crystal-clear pork bone broth, garnished with spring onions and sesame oil.
instructions: Make broth: simmer pork bones and dried shrimp in 2L water for 25 minutes. Season, strain.
Mix filling: pork, shrimp, ginger, soy sauce, wine, sesame oil, white pepper, and half the spring onions.
Place a teaspoon of filling on a wonton wrapper. Fold into a triangle, then bring two corners together and press to seal.
Boil wontons in plain water 3–4 minutes until they float and are cooked through. Don't cook in the broth.
Divide hot broth into bowls. Add wontons and bok choy.
Drizzle sesame oil, scatter spring onions, add white pepper. Serve immediately.
tips: Cook wontons in separate water — they cloud the broth. A good clear broth is the mark of a well-made wonton soup.

name: General Tso's Chicken
description: An American-Chinese culinary icon — crispy fried chicken tossed in a sweet, sticky, mildly spicy sauce with dried chillies. Invented in New York in the 1970s and now universally beloved.
instructions: Marinate chicken in 1 tbsp soy sauce and egg for 15 minutes. Coat in cornflour.
Fry in 180°C oil until golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels.
Mix sauce: remaining soy sauce, hoisin, vinegar, sugar, stock, and sesame oil.
In a wok, stir-fry garlic, ginger, and dried chillies in 1 tbsp oil for 30 seconds.
Pour in sauce; bubble until slightly thickened.
Toss crispy chicken in the sauce to coat. Garnish with spring onions and sesame seeds. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Fry chicken in small batches to keep oil temperature up. The sauce should just coat the chicken — not drown it.

name: Red Bean Soup (Hong Dou Tang)
description: A soothing, lightly sweet Cantonese dessert soup of slow-simmered azuki beans with dried tangerine peel and rock sugar — served warm in winter and chilled in summer.
instructions: Drain soaked azuki beans. Place in a pot with 2L water and tangerine peel.
Bring to boil, skim foam. Simmer partially covered for 75 minutes until beans are very soft.
Add rock sugar in the last 15 minutes; stir until dissolved.
If using, cook glutinous rice balls separately in boiling water until they float; add to soup.
Serve warm or refrigerate and serve chilled. The soup thickens as it cools — add a little water when reheating.
tips: Don't add sugar until beans are fully cooked — sugar hardens the skin and prevents softening. Tangerine peel is the traditional finishing note.
